diary: Woke up in Sammamish to that bright, sunny Monday feeling and made coffee like a ritual—then did my quick pet-visit checklist before the day could get loud. By the time I hit the road, I was already thinking about leashes, treats, and those little hydration notes I never want to forget. Kirkland mornings went smoothly: Juanita Bay Park had that soft green smell, and I caught myself smiling while a dog chased a ball like it was their full-time job. I grabbed supplies at QFC in Totem Lake and hustled off to Marina Park for the longer play session. Downtown Seattle was the real shift—because of the early USA vs Wales match, the Pioneer Square bars were buzzing and lively even before noon. I kept checking the time like a metronome, made a quick dinner, then finished the evening drop-in back near Bridle Trails. Restocked at CVS late, finally home, and I just felt… steady. Like I’d earned the quiet.
thought_process: I planned my day around three constraints: where the bookings were clustered, the road-time between Kirkland and downtown, and my own need to stay calm so I don’t rush the dogs. The light rail closure between Westlake and University District didn’t hit me directly since I drove, but it still nudged me to leave a little earlier and assume downtown traffic could be odd. The World Cup match mattered too—not because it changed work, but because it changed the city’s energy. I used that buzz to justify grabbing lunch downtown and then stayed disciplined about time windows for the afternoon visits. My mood stayed even, mostly because every task had a clear end: walk, feed, notes, drive. Even the late restock at CVS felt like closure. By night, I felt satisfied rather than tired.
world_event_prompt: Sunny Monday in early June, high of 74F. FIFA World Cup group stage match (USA vs Wales) airs at 11am PDT — bars downtown open early. Light Rail closed between Westlake and University District 9am-3pm for maintenance.
